frontier_title: Monte Carlo Planning with Large Language Model for Text-Based Game Agents

residual_gaps.gap_text: The method relies on zero-shot LLM evaluation without any mechanism for LLM parameter adaptation, leading to brittleness when the LLM lacks domain knowledge or encounters out-of-distribution states.
residual_gaps.ancestor_assumption_blocking_it: Plan-Seq-Learn (2023) assumes the LLM can produce plausible high-level plans without task-specific fine-tuning or adaptation.
residual_gaps.why_evolution_did_not_close_it: The frontier's structural change (replacing sequential pipeline with memory-augmented MCTS) still uses the same pre-trained LLM for action evaluation; the evolution action did not include fine-tuning or adaptation of the LLM itself.
residual_gaps.why_not_resolved: The shared assumption that a pre-trained LLM suffices for zero-shot evaluation was not challenged by the frontier.

residual_gaps.gap_text: The MCTS-based exploration inherits high computational complexity without any pruning or approximation, limiting scalability to tasks with large branching factors or long horizons.
residual_gaps.ancestor_assumption_blocking_it: Everything of Thoughts (2023) assumes that full-breadth MCTS is computationally affordable for the given domain.
residual_gaps.why_evolution_did_not_close_it: The frontier's evolution action (data type) replaced domain-specific RL pretraining with memory augmentation but retained the same MCTS tree search procedure, thus not addressing the inherent computational cost.
residual_gaps.why_not_resolved: The MCTS component was carried over unchanged, preserving the computational bottleneck.

residual_gaps.gap_text: The approach uses only text-based LLM pattern recognition for world dynamics, lacking an explicit structured model for spatial or causal reasoning, which fails in environments requiring precise geometric or physical simulations.
residual_gaps.structural_property_name: world model fidelity for spatial reasoning
residual_gaps.ancestor_assumption_blocking_it: Ghost in the Minecraft (2023) assumes that text-based interaction and LLM pattern recognition suffice for modeling world dynamics, without a dedicated spatial or causal reasoning module.
residual_gaps.why_evolution_did_not_close_it: The frontier's structural change (from single-shot plan generation to memory-augmented MCTS) still operates entirely on text representations and does not introduce any explicit world model or simulation component, leaving the reliance on LLM pattern recognition unchanged.
residual_gaps.why_not_resolved: The text-only assumption was inherited without modification.

residual_gaps.gap_text: The method lacks any mechanism for learning fine-grained low-level control policies, as it discards the hierarchical RL component, limiting applicability to tasks requiring precise motor commands or continuous control.
residual_gaps.ancestor_assumption_blocking_it: RL-GPT (background) assumes that high-level actions can be executed via pre-defined code and that low-level policies can be trained via RL, but the frontier's removal of the RL component eliminates this capability.
residual_gaps.why_evolution_did_not_close_it: The frontier's structural change replaced the hierarchical code+RL structure with a memory-augmented MCTS, which does not include any RL component, thus abandoning the ability to learn low-level control policies.
residual_gaps.why_not_resolved: The RL component was removed entirely, leaving no substitute for low-level policy learning.

residual_gaps.gap_text: The method assumes a fixed, known set of valid actions at each step, inherited from the neurosymbolic reasoner approach, and does not address scenarios with dynamic or unknown action sets typical in real-world planning.
residual_gaps.structural_property_name: action set flexibility under dynamic conditions
residual_gaps.ancestor_assumption_blocking_it: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners assumes the environment provides a list of valid actions at each decision point, which the agent uses for action selection.
residual_gaps.why_evolution_did_not_close_it: The frontier's structural change (adding memory) did not modify the assumption about action spaces; it still operates under the same precondition that valid actions are enumerated, thus the property remains unaddressed.
residual_gaps.why_not_resolved: The assumption of static valid action sets was inherited without challenge.

residual_gaps.gap_text: The method depends on the pre-trained LLM's initial action evaluations without any mechanism to correct systematic biases; the memory module can only accumulate experience but not revise the LLM's inherent knowledge gaps.
residual_gaps.ancestor_assumption_blocking_it: Large Language Models as Commonsense Knowledge for Large-Scale Task Planning (2023) assumes the LLM's induced world model and policy are sufficiently accurate for providing prior for MCTS.
residual_gaps.why_evolution_did_not_close_it: The frontier's evolution action (reasoning_pattern) replaced static world model and policy with memory-augmented evaluations, but the evaluations still originate from the same pre-trained LLM; the memory does not correct inherent LLM biases, only adjusts scores based on past outcomes.
residual_gaps.why_not_resolved: The dependence on LLM's implicit knowledge was not broken.

residual_gaps.gap_text: The method still uses a pre-trained LLM zero-shot without fine-tuning, making it brittle to domain shifts where the LLM's training distribution does not cover the target task.
residual_gaps.ancestor_assumption_blocking_it: LgTS (background) assumes that the LLM can generate appropriate sub-goals without any domain-specific fine-tuning.
residual_gaps.why_evolution_did_not_close_it: The frontier's structural change (from static sub-goal generation to memory-augmented MCTS) does not involve any LLM fine-tuning or adaptation; it inherits the same reliance on the pre-trained LLM.
residual_gaps.why_not_resolved: The reliance on zero-shot LLM performance was inherited.

residual_gaps.gap_text: The method assumes the pre-trained LLM has reasonable initial knowledge of the domain; if the LLM lacks domain familiarity, the memory-augmented evaluations may still fail to reach effective policies.
residual_gaps.ancestor_assumption_blocking_it: How Can LLM Guide RL? assumes that a fixed LLM policy prior provides useful regularization, relying on the LLM's domain knowledge.
residual_gaps.why_evolution_did_not_close_it: The frontier's structural change (from static regularization to memory-augmented MCTS) still depends on the same pre-trained LLM's evaluations; memory can only refine existing knowledge, not acquire fundamentally new domain knowledge.
residual_gaps.why_not_resolved: The dependency on LLM's prior domain knowledge was not addressed.

shared_residual_gaps.gap_text: Multiple branches (A, D, F, G, H) rely on a pre-trained LLM for zero-shot action evaluation or planning without any mechanism for LLM adaptation, leading to brittleness under distribution shift or when the LLM lacks domain knowledge.
shared_residual_gaps.common_assumption: All these branches assume that a pre-trained LLM can provide useful action evaluations or plans without task-specific fine-tuning or adaptation of its internal parameters.

shared_residual_gaps.gap_text: Branches C and E, along with the composite synthesis, assume a fixed, known set of valid actions at each step, precluding dynamic action discovery or handling of unknown actions in open-world scenarios.
shared_residual_gaps.structural_property_name: action set closure under dynamic change
shared_residual_gaps.common_assumption: These branches assume the environment provides a static enumeration of valid actions, which the agent simply selects from, without need for action discovery or generation.

recent_actions.from_title: Plan-Seq-Learn: Language Model Guided RL for Solving Long Horizon Robotics Tasks
recent_actions.what_changed: A replaces B's sequential pipeline (LLM → motion planner → RL) with an integrated tree search that uses memory-augmented LLM evaluations to guide exploration and adapt plans across trials.

recent_actions.from_title: Everything of Thoughts: Defying the Law of Penrose Triangle for Thought Generation
recent_actions.what_changed: B injects domain knowledge via pretrained RL, while A relies on LLM memory augmentation for dynamic evaluation without training.

recent_actions.from_title: Ghost in the Minecraft: Generally Capable Agents for Open-World Environments via Large Language Models with Text-based Knowledge and Memory
recent_actions.what_changed: B uses a single-shot LLM plan generator with static memory; A replaces this with an MCTS loop where LLM evaluations are dynamically adjusted via in-trial and cross-trial memory.

recent_actions.from_title: RL-GPT: Integrating Reinforcement Learning and Code-as-policy (background)
recent_actions.what_changed: A drops the hierarchical code+RL structure in favor of tree search with memory-augmented LLM evaluations, eliminating the need for RL-based low-level refinement.

recent_actions.from_title: Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners
recent_actions.what_changed: A replaces B's static symbolic module with dynamic memory-augmented evaluation, allowing the LLM to adapt based on historical outcomes rather than relying on fixed symbolic rules.

recent_actions.from_title: Large Language Models as Commonsense Knowledge for Large-Scale Task Planning
recent_actions.what_changed: A replaces B's static LLM-induced world model and policy with dynamically updated memory-augmented evaluations, enabling the LLM to refine its action scores based on historical experience within and across games.

frontier_solves: The primary method is MC-DML, which enhances LLMs with dynamic memory for in-trial and cross-trial learning. Core components: LLM for action evaluation, tree search for exploration, and memory modules to adapt evaluations based on past experiences. This improves planning without multiple iterations.
frontier_method: The algorithm uses an LLM to evaluate actions during MCTS, with memory mechanisms that store and retrieve past outcomes. In-trial memory tracks immediate history within a game, while cross-trial memory accumulates knowledge across games. The LLM's evaluations are dynamically adjusted based on memory

composite_signal.what_A_combines: A takes MCTS for exploration from pred[1] (Everything of Thoughts), memory modules from pred[2] (Ghost in the Minecraft) but makes them dynamic, and LLM action evaluation from pred[4] (Large Language Models as Commonsense Knowledge for Large-Scale Task Planning).
composite_signal.obviousness_reason: While MCTS and memory are individually known, integrating them with LLM evaluations in a way that dynamically updates action scores based on in-trial and cross-trial memory requires solving a non-trivial interface problem: how to retrieve r
composite_signal.novel_combination: The novel property is the ability to adapt LLM evaluations based on accumulated experience (memory) within and across games, enabling the LLM to improve planning without multiple iterative calls or fine-tuning.
composite_signal.composite_remaining_gap: The method assumes a fixed, known set of valid actions at each step and relies on the quality of the LLM's implicit knowledge; it does not address scenarios with dynamic action sets or learning new actions.

frontier_what_persists: Both methods assume the LLM (without task-specific fine-tuning) can produce plausible plans or action evaluations for the target domain, and neither attempts to learn or adapt the LLM itself.
Both assume problems can be decomposed into sequential actions (text-based games) and rely on MCTS for exploration.
Both methods assume a text-only environment with predefined action sets, no structured world model beyond what the LLM captures from language.
Both A and B rely on pre-trained LLMs for decision-making without addressing the potential for catastrophic forgetting or distribution shift when the task domain changes significantly.
A inherits B's assumption that the agent has access to a list of valid actions at each decision point, which may not hold in many real-world planning tasks.
A inherits B's assumption that the LLM can provide useful zero-shot evaluations without task-specific training; both methods depend on the quality of the LLM's implicit knowledge.
Both A and B assume the LLM can provide useful guidance without any domain-specific fine-tuning or grounding.
A inherits B's assumption that an LLM with relevant domain knowledge is available to provide initial action evaluations.
